g.data-narration: Welcome to learning about Sparse Matrix Representation using Linked Lists. This is Module 1 continuation.
A sparse matrix has mostly zero elements. Storing all elements wastes memory since most values are zero.
Array triplet representation has limitations like fixed size and O(n) insertion. Linked lists solve these problems dynamically.

g.text: • Fixed size - must know max non-zero elements
• No direct column access - scan entire array
• Direct row AND column access via headers

g.data-narration: Each non-zero element is stored as a node with five fields: row, col, value, next_row, and next_col pointers.
The C struct defines all five fields. Next_row goes down a column, next_col goes across a row.
The multilist representation creates two linked lists per non-zero element - one horizontal via next_col, one vertical via next_row.

g.text: Each non-zero element belongs to TWO lists simultaneously:

g.data-narration: Header nodes anchor each row and column. Row headers have next_col pointing to first element in that row.

g.text: Row Headers: row_head[0], row_head[1], row_head[2], row_head[3]
→ row_head[r].next_col → first non-zero in row r
→ col_head[c].next_row → first non-zero in col c
row_head[0] points to (0,2,3) - first in row 0
col_head[2] points to (0,2,3) - first in column 2

g.data-narration: For our example matrix, Row 0 has elements at columns 2 and 4, linked via next_col. Column 2 has elements at rows 0, 1, 3.
g.text: Visualizing the Multilist for Our Matrix

g.data-narration: Insertion into the linked list maintains sorted order. For row insertion, we traverse by column. For column, traverse by row.
Transpose swaps rows and columns by iterating through original row lists and inserting as (col, row, value) into new matrix.

g.text: • Array transpose needs O(cols) per element

g.data-narration: Matrix addition merges two row lists, handling three cases: element in A only, B only, or both (add values).

g.text: If adding A + B where B has
sum may cancel to zero → skip

g.data-narration: Array triplet is simpler for static matrices. Linked list is better for dynamic data and when you need column-wise access.
